data.title: The Biggest Risk In BDCs, And How I Mitigate It As A Bull
data.description:
The current bear case for BDCs revolves around cockroaches, the SaaSacalopyse, and soaring defaults.

The liquidity gating from the largest private credit managers such as Blue Owl Capital (OWL), BlackRock (BLK) and now Ares Management (ARES) is what helps the bear case keep boiling.

However, as I have articulated it in several of my previous BDC macro level articles (e.g., Cockroaches, SaaSpocalypse, And Now 'GFC 2.0' and Stop Panicking And Start Buying BDCs) there is no evidence that would even come close to justifying the current BDC discounts.

In a nutshell:

  • Cockroaches - First Brands and Tricolor - had nothing to do with direct lending but with broadly syndicated loan market.
  • BDC non-accruals and PIK level remain very low and exhibit no signs of material deterioration.
  • Not all BDCs carry ~20% SaaS portfolio exposures and most SaaS loans are senior secured first lien with loan-to-value below 50% or below.
  • Liquidity gating is fully logical step given private credit's liquidity profile (just as for private real estate, infrastructure projects etc.). Plus, it is only logical that investors sell private BDCs at or close to NAV (because that is what is offered by the managers) if they see publicly traded BDCs trading at ~30% below NAV. A wonderful arbitrage - sell high, buy low.

Trust me, I could make this list much longer, but debunking BDC bear myths is not my intention here (for that I suggest exploring the two articles I referenced above).

Instead, my goal of this piece is to do the opposite.

What I would like to do is to provide a nuanced and potentially deadly weapon that could be used by the BDC bears to keep the headlines hot.

It is also the main risk, which makes me extra careful pertaining to where and in what BDCs I deploy my capital.

The risk

In essence, the end result of this key risk materializing is higher default rates, leading to NAV destruction and dividend cuts.

Here is a 4-step path which could lead to this.

First, redemptions continue to remain elevated until the pent-up demand gets fully satisfied, forcing private BDCs to shrink their portfolios.

Given how fancy it has become to argue against private credit and considering the current daylight between private BDC and public BDC NAVs I think that this is a likely scenario.

Second, public BDCs continue to trade below NAV which in the context of already exhausted balance sheets and very tight dividend coverage levels leaves them dry of capital.

Third, private credit BDC loans start to come due as planned and thus have to be refinanced (which is a typical process) in situation, when BDCs don't have spare dry powder (instead, they have to think about capturing those principals and repaying back fleeing investors).

Fourth, these private credit loan investment companies are thus forced to find alternative lenders, which for sure will be demanding higher risk premiums, thereby introducing potentially unsustainable pressures on the company cash flows.

The worst what could happen is that highly leveraged companies don't get refinanced at all (because no other lenders would be willing to lean into such positions), leading to an automatic default.

Now, the BDC bulls (including myself) could try to destroy this logic by

(a) reminding us that private credit BDCs and their liquidity issues are totally unrelated to publicly traded BDCs which offer liquidity via exchange,

(b) stating that 5% redemption cap is such for a reason as it corresponds to a typical private loan duration of 5 years (i.e., 20% portfolio gets turned over per year which is 5% per quarter).

However, I've to say that these are quite weak arguments.

To address the (a) part.

The reality is that many BDCs are intertwined, especially those that are managed by the same sponsor or alternative asset manager franchise.

OWL and its two BDCs: the public one Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) and the private one Blue Owl Capital Corporation II are great examples.

There are huge overlaps between these two BDCs in terms of what portfolio companies comprise their assets.

I have done a quick experiment by taking the first seven (Monotype, Peraton, STS PARENT, AAM, Hg Genesis, Associations Finance) portfolio loan investment holdings that are reflected in OBDC II 10-k filing and tried to find them in OBDC's 10-k filing. All were perfect hits.

The same could be said about BLK's BCRED (the largest private BDC) and badly performing BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC) - see TCPC's 10-k filing here.

This is a structural feature which is relevant across the board when it comes to platform-derived BDCs (i.e., BDCs that fall under the same umbrella name).

On top of that, there are even shared loan investments that go beyond the same "platform". The overlaps are not as strong, but still, they do exist.

For example, by selecting random 10 names from OBDC's portfolio and trying to find them in Ares Capital (ARCC) 10-k filing I got three hits: Aptean, Balrog Acquisition and Pluralsight. In fact, Pluralsight is something that we can find in Oaktree Specialty Lending (OCSL) and TCPC portfolios.

So, the risk is that if one of these chain elements breaks (e.g., private BDCs unable to refinance), the end-result of my 4-step process could indeed materialize.

To address the (b) part.

I will be quick here.

The simple reality is that a decent chunk of BDC loan investments do not end with their maturity dates. In other words, quite many get amended and/or extended, which rejects the notion of private BDCs being able to match their redemptions with loan repayments.

Granted, in many instances the businesses after LBOs have managed to deleverage and find lenders on better terms, say, in the public debt markets or banks.

However, this cannot be applied to all portfolio holdings.

I have again taken a closer look at OBDC's holdings list by comparing loan terms that were present in 2022 10-k filing with those that we can find in the most recent 2025 10-k. I started with letter "A" company names and went down until I got 4 hits (see the differences in loan maturity dates between 2022 and now):

  • Armstrong Bidco Limited 6/2025 vs. 6/2029
  • Associations, Inc 6/2024 vs. 7/2028
  • BCTO BSI 12/2026 vs. 12/2028
  • Blackhawk Network Holdings, Inc. 6/2026 vs. 3/2029

How to manage it

I see three ways to mitigate this (potential) risk.

1. To gravitate away from upper middle market lending space in which loan ticket sizes are so large that they have to be shared across multiple lenders in order to avoid single-borrower concentration risk.

In the core middle market and especially in the lower middle market segments the loan ticket sizes are much smaller and thus decrease dependency on other lenders.

2. To de-emphasize allocations to BDCs that are operated by large-scale managers, which are exposed to their private BDC redemptions requests.

Publicly traded BDCs, which don't have private sister BDCs should not have the investment cross-sharing risks sitting on the same platform.

3. To stay away from low quality BDCs with limited capital availability, which might not have the resources to refinance or upsize their loan investments if such need arises.

The debt to equity of 1.1x (or lower) should be sufficient to manage this risk. The P/NAV above 1.0x which is mostly relevant for internally managed BDCs could help secure fresh equity financing on accretive terms as well.

Two of my Top 3 BDC picks - Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) and Capital Southwest (CSWC) - tick all of the boxes almost perfectly (see my ticker-specific articles here and here, respectively).

They are both internally managed, which means that they are not dependent or influenced by larger asset managers. It also supports their P/NAV metrics, which currently are above 1.0x, offering access to accretive equity capital.

Neither TRIN nor CSWC play in the upper middle market space. They don't even lend to core middle market companies. TRIN's focus is on high-quality venture stage and equipment finance opportunities. CSWC is, however, a pure-play lower middle market lender, where again loan ticket sizes and general loan terms support robust credit risk management.

For TRIN and CSWC the exposure to SaaS is below 10% and 5% of their portfolios, respectively, meaning that they are not materially exposed to "hated" SaaS sector in which potential refinancing opportunities might be challenged (and non-accruals could potentially inflate from this niche).

Final words

Finally, let me conclude by saying that even this liquidity-driven credit risk that I outlined above seems like a low probability even in my eyes.

The average debt to equity for private BDCs is 0.94x, which offers sizeable access to additional liquidity (debt) to meet the redemptions in case through organic loan repayments it is not possible. Similarly, based on recent examples it is clear that there is a "NAV-aligned" market to sell parts of the portfolio (without fire-sale discounts). In the worst case, the alternative asset managers themselves could step in with the necessary fire power.

In a nutshell, I remain extremely bullish on BDC and continue to allocate my incremental portfolio contributions right in this space.

data.title: SPMO: Not So Much Momentum Now, But That's What Makes It A Buy Long Term
data.description:
Introduction

Due to heightened volatility, the stock market, particularly the S&P (SP500), has lost its momentum from 2025, where it saw gains over 16%.

While I expect this to continue for at least the near to medium term, now is the time I believe investors should start to get greedy.

While I understand investor concerns, I've always been riskier and believe volatility is a long-term buying opportunity. With market volatility increasing in recent months, I believe the market is presenting some attractive, long-term buying opportunities.

However, the Invesco S&P 500 Momentum ETF (SPMO) has a real chance of underperforming, at least for the near term due to rising geopolitical risks.

In this article, I discuss their performance, recent changes after the semi-annual rebalancing, and why long-term investors should consider this ETF as a core holding going forward.

Previous Buy Rating

I covered SPMO this past October, rating the ETF a buy due to its strong performance. Their reasonable expense ratio of 0.13% and outperformance vs. ETF stalwarts over a one- and three-year period made them ideal as a core holding. Since then, the ETF has underperformed the S&P itself, down 5.32% compared to 1.31% for the index.

Performance

As previously mentioned, SPMO's momentum has been halted by increased volatility, mainly due to the ongoing war with Iran. While there have been talks about a ceasefire, Iran stated they were not interested in accepting one.

This means that SPMO could continue underperforming until the two sides come to an agreement. When will that be? No one knows, but I'm sure something will get worked out in the future.

Until then, investors should expect muted gains for SPMO. But for long-term investors, that's a buying opportunity.

Last year, SPMO vastly outperformed notable ETFs like the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD), the Vanguard 500 Index Fund ETF (VOO), and iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) in total returns.

Through the first three months of 2026, the tables have turned, with the dividend-focused ETFs leading the way. SCHD is up double digits thanks to their concentration in the Energy (XLE) sector.

SPMO and VOO's underperformance are nearly identical due to them tracking the S&P. And if the war continues, it's likely SPMO will continue to underperform.

Year-to-date, the ETF has seen minimal NAV growth, which has impacted its performance. Through the first 3 months or so, SPMO has experienced less than 1% NAV growth.

But as a long-term investor, it's essential to look at NAV growth over time. The higher NAV growth an ETF sees, the higher the likelihood it will have better returns.

Over a 1, 3, 5, and 10-year time frame, SPMO has seen double-digit NAV growth. And since SPMO focuses on stocks with increased momentum, they're much more likely to perform well over the long term.

Recent Changes

For those unfamiliar, SPMO rebalances semi-annually. This happens on the 3rd Friday in both March and September. And the ETF made some notable changes during its last rebalancing.

Below are their top 10 holdings after their recent rebalancing.

Notable changes include the removal of Meta Platforms (META), Palantir Technologies (PLTR), Walmart (WMT), Oracle Corp. (ORCL), General Electric Company (GE), Visa (V), Netflix (NFLX), & JPMorgan Chase (JPM) from its top 10 holdings.

NVIDIA Corp. (NVDA) and Broadcom (AVGO) are the only two that remain after the recent rebalancing. The two companies actually switched spots as AVGO was the largest position, accounting for 9.76% of the portfolio.

PLTR is still within the portfolio but now accounts for 1.80% compared to 4.58% prior. I'm a bit disappointed that Visa was removed completely, as I consider them one of the highest quality dividend growth stocks today.

Peer Mastercard (MA) is also not in the portfolio. But SPMO still has notable dividend payers like Altria (MO), The Coca-Cola Company (KO), Phillip Morris International (PM), and REIT Welltower (WELL).

Higher Distribution Than Last Year

Speaking of dividends, SPMO announced a higher distribution compared to previous ones. Their latest distribution of $0.3211 represents a year-over-year increase of 79%.

I believe part of this may be to increase investor interest since the ETF has seen higher volatility in recent months.

As I discussed in my prior article, the distributions are less tax favorable since they're considered ordinary income. As a result, they're better held inside a tax favorable account like a Roth IRA.

How Will SPMO Perform Going Forward?

Honestly, I think it's possible for SPMO to see muted returns in 2026. Especially if the war continues with no end in sight. Throw in the possibility of sending in ground troops, and I believe it's possible for the ETF to see negative returns for 2026.

But over the long term, I think SPMO will continue to do well due to its momentum strategy. Furthermore, the higher exposure to the Technology (XLK) sector should bode well due to the likelihood of seeing rapid AI integration.

While investors have rotated out of the Technology sector in favor of dividend stocks, it's possible SPMO will shift towards more dividend heavy sectors in the near future.

But as mentioned previously, the rapid adoption of AI is expected to continue for the foreseeable future. And this is why I remain confident in SPMO as a long-term core holding.

I think for the near term, AI heavy funds may see potential underperformance due to fears of AI disruption in the labor market. But over the next eight years, AI growth is projected to be strong in North America.

Risks & Investor Takeaway

As discussed, a major risk for SPMO is underperformance. Although the ETF has outperformed pure growth ETFs, likely due to their adaptability, economic uncertainty remains a huge risk.

If the war with Iran continues, I suspect more volatility and the potential to see negative returns, at least for the near term. A longer war also increases the risk of a recession and higher for longer inflation.

If so, it's possible for SPMO to underperform as investors rotate into dividend-focused ETFs like SCHD & DGRO due to their higher yields.

But long-term I remain bullish on SPMO due to the fund's adaptability and higher technology exposure. If you're looking for a core holding in your retirement portfolio, then consider Invesco's S&P 500 Momentum ETF.

data.title: The Truth Social Posts Are Losing Their Impact
data.description:
I feel like I need a degree in psychology and military tactics, along with my investment experience, to navigate this market. In the ongoing war of words, President Trump rebuked Iran’s dismissal of his 15-point plan by saying, “They are begging to make a deal, not me. They’re begging to make a deal.” Investors were not enthused, as stocks tumbled, oil prices rose sharply, and Treasury yields surged back to last Friday’s highs, with the 2-year reaching 4% and the 10-year climbing back to 4.42%. The bond market seems to be the tipping point for the Trump administration, because after the close, the President blinked with another Truth Social post.

“As per the Iranian government’s request, please let this statement serve to represent that I am pausing the period of Energy Plant destruction by 10 days to Monday, April 6.” He followed that by asserting that negotiations were ongoing and going very well. Initially, stocks had a more muted response in the futures market, rebounding some 30 S&P 500 points, which was a much weaker reaction than what we saw on Monday morning following the initial extension. This morning, we are back in the red again with rising oil prices and Treasury yields. That’s a concern because it means the President can no longer jawbone the markets. Investors are demanding tangible de-escalation at this point, as the economic risks mount.

If President Trump were to follow through with his threat to destroy Iran’s energy sites, decapitating the country’s primary source of revenue, it would severely undercut his promise to help the Iranian people who have been oppressed by the regime. In fact, it could turn the population against us and strengthen the stranglehold that the regime maintains.

It would also lead to a surge in oil and gas prices well above what we have seen in recent weeks, and if Iran retaliated with its seemingly endless supply of drones by destroying more energy infrastructure in neighboring countries, the price surge could be parabolic. I find it hard to believe that the other Arab states, several of which have committed to investing huge sums in the U.S., would look on this favorably. Furthermore, if their primary source of revenue was diminished, so too would be the promised investments.

My critics will say I am being too practical, but I think everything boils down to economics when it comes to decisions like this, and aside from ego, I see zero economic upside for the President by following through with his threats. This still appears to be a form of intimidation to force an agreement, which is backed up by the increasing U.S. military presence in the region. Again, I think the bond market is what will force the Trump administration to reconsider. The 2-year yield is now at 4.01% as of this writing, which is a full 50 basis points above the Fed Funds rate.

On the domestic front, initial weekly unemployment claims rose 5,000 yesterday to 210,000 but remain near their lowest level in the past year, while continuing claims fell 32,000 to their lowest level since May 2024. Despite the recent layoff announcement, we are still in a low-hire, low-fire labor market.

As for consumer spending, the rise in gasoline prices has yet to impact activity, as the latest Redbook Retail Sales report shows spending at some 9,000 general merchandise stores rose 6.7% over the past year in the week ending March 24. Sentiment is abysmal, but consumers are maintaining spending levels. I am reluctant to change my outlook until I see real-time economic indicators deteriorating further, given that this geopolitical conflict could end as abruptly and suddenly as it started a month ago.

I still find it unconscionable that the President would undermine his entire economic agenda by following through on threats that would lead to even more supply chain disruption, a stronger dollar, higher energy costs, higher inflation, higher interest rates, and an increasingly perturbed electorate that does not want boots on the ground risking American lives in the Middle East. This is why I suspect he will frame an abrupt exit as a victory and de-escalate before his next deadline arrives. As I said from the start, markets will ultimately dictate policy.

Given that the S&P 500 broke its long-term uptrend, the March decline is not necessarily a buying opportunity. The market will likely remain choppy over the next couple of months as investors weigh the damage to the domestic economy. That said, the Nasdaq Composite and Russell 2000 have both corrected 10% from their all-time highs, while the small-cap index and the equally weighted S&P 500 have held their long-term trend lines. I think we have exercised a lot of the speculation and excessive valuations out of this market. By some measures, we are reaching oversold levels, with the percentage of stocks in the S&P 500 trading above their 50-day moving average falling to just 21%.

Furthermore, CNN’s Fear & Greed Index is closer to where it bottoms, suggesting that a trough in the market is getting closer. If yesterday’s market action is repeated today, I would expect a response from the President that has more meaning than the one we saw after yesterday’s close, before Monday morning’s open.

data.title: WTI Crude Oil Minor Pullback Over, Start Of New Bullish Leg For Breakout Above $102.25
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

This is a follow-up analysis and an update of our prior report, “Chart alert: WTI crude oil rally almost reached $102.25, risk of minor setback towards $88.36,” published on 16 March 2026.

The price actions of the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) have staged the expected minor corrective pullback of 17% from its key $102.25 near-term range resistance after a retest on Monday, 23 March 2026, at the start of the London session to hit an intraday low of $85.50 at mid-London session on the same day due to US President Trump’s “optimistic claims” that US and Iran are in the process of negotiating an immediate ceasefire deal.

Thereafter, the West Texas Oil CFD traded sideways above its 20-day moving average as Iran rejected the US's ceasefire proposal and continued to strike the Gulf states’ key installations as the US-Iran war entered its 28th day.

In addition, conflicting messages are being sent out from the US White House Administration. US President Trump has extended by 10 days his pledge to refrain from attacks on Iranian energy-producing sites and added that the “negotiation talks are going very well”.

On the other hand, the Wall Street Journal has reported that the Pentagon is considering sending additional troops, as many as 10,000, to the Middle East, on top of the already deployed 2,000 soldiers from the 82nd Airborne Division. Hence, increasing the odds of a US ground invasion into Iranian soil as soon as this weekend.

Right now, several technical factors are suggesting the potential start of a new bullish up move sequence for West Texas crude oil at this juncture.

WTI calendar spread remains in negative territory

The WTI crude oil calendar spread, defined as the difference between the 12-month forward price and the spot price, serves as a gauge of the market’s structural conditions.

A positive spread (contango) reflects a typical environment where futures trade at a premium to spot, factoring in storage and transportation costs.

In contrast, a negative spread (backwardation) signals near-term supply tightness, with strong prompt demand pushing spot prices above futures.

At the time of writing, the spread has deepened into backwardation at -21.74, marking its most negative level since 6 March 2026 (-23.92) on the onset of the ongoing US-Iran war (see Fig. 1).

Hence, near-term prices of WTI crude oil are likely to face further upside pressure.

Let’s switch our attention to the potential short-term trajectory (1 to 3 days) of WTI crude oil.

WTI Crude Oil - Imminent potential bullish breakout above $102.25 minor range top

The minor price structure of the West Texas Oil CFD (a proxy for WTI crude oil futures) has turned more constructive over the past four sessions, supported by its ability to hold above a rising 20-day moving average.

On Thursday, 26 March 2026, it has cleared above its $93.70 near-term resistance and is en route to retest its $102.25 minor range resistance in place since 16 March 2026.

Watch the $88.36/85.50 key short-term pivotal support to maintain the current bullish momentum, and a clearance above $102.25 increases the odds of a fresh bullish impulsive up move sequence to seek out the next intermediate resistances at $111.28, $116.56/119.54, and $124.40 (see Fig. 2).

However, a break and an hourly close below $85.50 invalidates the bullish scenario for an extension of the minor corrective decline to expose the next intermediate supports at $81.85, $77.26/76.83, and $73.38 (also the 50-day moving average).

Key elements to support the bullish bias on WTI crude oil

  • Price actions remain above its rising 20-day and 50-day moving averages, which indicates that the medium-term uptrend phase remains intact.
  • The hourly MACD trend indicator has just flashed out a bullish crossover condition above its centreline. This latest positive observation has occurred above its prior bullish breakout above its former key descending resistance on Thursday, 26 March 2026. An indication of a change of trend in the West Texas Oil CFD, from sideways to a minor bullish trend.

data.title: The Tesla Robotaxi Story Is A Myth: Why I'm Maintaining My Strong Sell Into Q1 Earnings
data.description:
I was recently asked which was the best robotaxi stock play for 2026, here on Seeking Alpha, and I broke down a list of companies with a working operation and/or a clear path to monetization. I noticed a few of you in the comments asking why Tesla, Inc. (TSLA), didn't even make the list, almost as if I could forget the biggest "bull case" in the industry.

I can assure you, I didn’t forget.

In fact, Tesla deserves its own dedicated analysis to explain exactly why I excluded it from my top picks (or, for that matter, just picks) for autonomous mobility exposure. While I see some news, which surprises me over and over again, like recent Morgan Stanley (MS) analysts claiming Tesla has an enormous positive catalyst for unsupervised ride miles (Alphabet's (GOOG)(GOOGL) Waymo from almost 6 years ago does a “facepalm” when it reads it), I see a company that is still fundamentally struggling to prove that its approach is safe enough for a driverless future. I am genuinely surprised there are people, who are willing to risk their lives to test out CEO Elon Musk's safety-narrative.

The core of my skepticism comes from the technological divide I discussed in my recent Mobileye (MBLY) article. Mobileye is a company that has been a leader in autonomy for cars, and was originally supposed to provide Tesla Autopilot, but the partnership came to an abrupt end due to a safety dispute in 2016. Mobileye management works on an advanced system, which will be able to determine the best possible behavior for a driverless car in any critical situation.

I bring it up in this article for an argument that Mobileye's management made. They say that thousands of hours of video will never “teach” the neural network how to respond in the infinite number of scenarios, which can happen on the road, some of which may never repeat again. Mobileye, for example, uses a "fast and slow" architecture that mimics human reflexes and deep cognitive processing, and they say that miles collected are an incredibly slow way to teach a car.

Mobileye began collecting data way before Tesla, by the way. It had 200 PB of data in 2022, but since then, the company has stopped disclosing it, the same as the miles collected, because the market realized that data doesn’t get them closer to safe driverless technology. Other competitors like Alphabet also stated repeatedly that relying on a single sensing modality will not bring a sufficient level of safety.

Meanwhile, Tesla continues to insist that cameras are enough, and of course, there are always big headlines about the miles Tesla has collected, like the latest 8.5 billion-mile milestone, even though they aren’t even autonomous miles, but rather supervised miles collected from Tesla owners all over the world. Even if the data is enough, the quality of Tesla’s data falls way behind the data from Waymo, which was collected directly by the driverless cars.

We don't have to guess who is right because we have the data from Austin. Austin was the first city where Tesla’s Level 2++ was deployed, meeting Waymo’s Level 4 robotaxis. The results show everything you need to know about Tesla. The company is best at what is unimportant. For example, it wins "most miles driven," but only Waymo was able to scale to 200 fully autonomous taxis, while Tesla is stuck with road tests in small, geofenced areas. Out of 43 vehicles Tesla has deployed in Austin, only 8 are unsupervised, and even these 8 are believed to be supervised at a distance.

I have to say, I am really concerned about how little data those companies are required to share. It’s absolutely outrageous that a vehicle without the Level 4 clearance is allowed to be “unsupervised,” even in a geofenced area.

And thankfully, it seems I am not the only one concerned over this lack of transparency, and "cutting of corners" when it comes to rules. This month, the NHTSA started an engineering analysis into Tesla’s Full Self-Driving (FSD) system, covering over 3 million vehicles. The probe is specifically looking at the system's "degradation detection," or rather, its failure to detect it. Regulators are concerned that Tesla’s vision-only approach cannot handle poor roadway conditions like sun glare, fog, or dust (as a matter of fact, there are many videos online proving it). The NHTSA even noted cases where FSD completely lost track of or never even detected a vehicle in its path due to visibility issues:

NHTSA cited several crashes, including one fatal, in which the system failed to adequately warn drivers in low-visibility conditions such as sun glare, fog, or dust. The investigation, which began in 2024, is now moving to an “engineering analysis,” a deeper review that could lead to a recall or other enforcement action.

While Elon Musk has promised for almost a decade now a fully autonomous vehicle, the data we have today and intensifying skepticism from analysts and experts, including probes, suggest otherwise. Also, as Tesla stops Model S and X production and wants to make the "Optimus" robot factory instead, it feels like the company is just jumping from one unfulfilled promise to the next.

Tesla Robotaxi Progress

We are now nine months into the Austin launch, and Tesla has a tiny fleet of about 40 vehicles, even though in October 2025, Musk promised "500 or more" robotaxis in the Greater Austin area by the end of the year. Based on crowdsourced data, the availability of these cars is often below 20%, and of course, as I already said, they are mostly not unsupervised. To put that in perspective, Waymo is operating over 2,500 fully driverless (they are unsupervised; it turns out it is possible with the right technology!) taxis across several U.S. cities. Waymo has over 127 million autonomous miles, and its research shows its crash rate is 85% lower than that of human drivers, and way lower than that of Tesla, which previously had a crash rate 4 times higher than that of human drivers. Also, Waymo’s data is official, provided by the company, and includes all of the details, including differences to the benchmark, the severity of damage, etc. Tesla doesn’t provide research like this, most likely, as the results would disappoint the believers.

If you think it will improve over time, for now, it doesn’t look like this. Based on the 15 crash incidents reported from June 2025 until March 2026, and the estimated mileage of the fleet, Tesla’s "robotaxis" are crashing roughly once every 57,000 miles. For the average American driver, that figure is one crash every 500,000 miles. This means Tesla’s supervised system is currently performing about 9 times worse than a human driver. And remember, these are incidents that happened even with a human supervisor present who is supposed to prevent them. Only 8 out of 43 cars are stated as “unsupervised.”

When I look at these numbers, I realize how right Mobileye is in its statement:

The sample complexity of policy is very, very high, again, because it's a multi-agent compounding effect, right? The host vehicle performs an action in the world and this action affects other road users. Therefore, there's this compounding effect. So when you have something of a very high complexity, you need a lot, a lot of data. Now the data coming from real world is limited. Even if you have millions of cars sending data, it's still limited. You can do -- you can run on a simulator, say, photorealistic simulators and do training over a simulator but then you are compute bound because then compute becomes a bottleneck.

And by the way, I am not Mobileye’s fangirl. I rated the stock as a Hold for now, but I really liked the fact that Mobileye is a tech-first company. The management there has a large academic background. I respect the company, and I trust their judgment, because to me it seems that studying and developing the technology is a goal number one for them, and not the business. It might not be the best fact from a shareholder’s perspective, but from analysts, I think it is very interesting.

Also, I am pretty cautious about the whole idea, adoption, and, most importantly, monetization of robotaxis. In Seeking Alpha Asks, I said:

Speaking of the robotaxi space, I’ve been skeptical about the wide-scale adoption of this technology since the beginning, and I remain skeptical today. For me, as a consumer, the fact that a car is driverless doesn’t provide any specific value if it is not significantly cheaper than a regular taxi service. Autonomous cars, which can free my hands and potentially free my attention while driving, could be a game changer, especially when we are stuck in traffic for hours. But this is not an advantage for robotaxis. For this reason, I would be investing in mobility, and if I wanted some robotaxi exposure, I’d choose names that focus on different types of mobility.

When I add to my concerns the lack of transparency from Tesla, I think it’s clear I can’t be bullish on this business segment of Tesla. While other autonomous companies, like Waymo, Amazon (AMZN) with its Zoox, or even Baidu (BIDU) with Apollo Go, provide full descriptions of what happened during their crashes to the NHTSA, Tesla redacts everything, marking it as "confidential business information," which means the public and researchers have no way of knowing what happened.

It’s also easy for Tesla to report "fewer crashes" in a month, as they did in February, without mentioning that due to the weather conditions, the fleet didn’t operate for several days, and also, February is a short month, so fewer miles equals fewer crashes. It’s not Tesla’s win. Also, without data about mileage, we can’t claim there was an improvement. If Tesla were truly confident that its tech is safe and working as expected, it wouldn't have anything to hide.

Before Q1

To make matters worse for the shareholders, the market braces as Tesla is heading into next week’s Q1 deliveries report. Analysts at UBS and RBC are cutting their forecasts, with some expecting deliveries to drop as low as 345,000 units, which would be a strong decline both sequentially and year-over-year.

By now, I know the main argument of the fans is that deliveries don't matter because Tesla is an "AI and Robotics" company. And you could be right, because except for the AI and robotics, Tesla doesn’t have any hopes for the future, but in reality, the automotive business funds the R&D for things like Optimus and the Cybercab, and everything else. If the core business continues to shrink, there will be no cash to wait for the new era of Tesla innovation to kick in.

As I mentioned in my previous article, Tesla is winding down Model S and X production to make space for robot factories, but all I see in this is that the demand for their premium vehicles is declining, and margins are being crushed by competition. In my previous article, you can also read how "Musk Premium" is being moved away from Tesla and toward his private ventures like SpaceX (SPACE) and xAI (X.AI). I think Elon recognizes that Tesla didn’t go as planned, so now Tesla shareholders will fund high-risk AI experiments to hopefully stop the company from crashing.

Conclusion

Writing this article feels a bit like describing a parallel reality. I have been breaking down financials and studying different management styles for a few years now, and while it’s not a lot, Tesla always seems to exist in a category of its own for me personally. I recognize that I have strong opinions on this, and it is perfectly fine if we disagree (that is what makes a market), but for me, the disconnect between the narrative and the facts is inconceivably enormous.

It is difficult to believe there are reports from major analyst firms claiming that supervised "miles driven" is an enormous catalyst for a robotaxi business that barely exists. I prefer to trust companies that provide extensive research and management teams that are focused on the technical side of their products rather than selling a vision of the future to the stock market. I understand that a "tech-first" approach doesn’t always lead to immediate stock gains, and we see that clearly with Mobileye being down 50% over the last year while Tesla’s valuation remains detached from its fundamentals.

The "Musk Premium" may stay intact today, or even for the next year, but eventually, every narrative has to face reality. Currently, that reality is not a pretty picture for Tesla, with a shrinking core business and compressed margins. I am not interested in joining a rally that is based only on sentiment, while the business model was based on first-mover advantage, curiosity of the market, Musk as a public figure, and not on real superiority beyond some initial developments with the first few car models.

While sentiment can help boost my profits, and I often use it, my investment philosophy has always been to prioritize the business. For these reasons, I am maintaining my Strong Sell rating on Tesla.

Non-diversified funds that focus on a relatively small number of stocks tend to be more volatile than diversified funds and the market as a whole.

Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Technology Portfolio (FSPTX) is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • We believe the value of technology stocks is in large part determined by the companies' future potential to generate earnings and cash flow.
  • Our investment framework also focuses on identifying themes that impact the largest end markets, determining potential winners/losers, and how certain companies that are technology disruptors can impact incumbents.
  • The technology sector is a very specialized part of the market, and our experience allows for proficiency in specific domains, aiding in recognizing investment opportunities when they arise.
  • Through bottom-up research and by leveraging Fidelity's vast expertise – in addition to insights from industry experts, technologists, suppliers and competitors – we develop a differentiated view on the fundamentals in seeking to identify companies with compelling risk/reward profiles.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

Market Review

For the fourth quarter of 2025, the information technology sector gained 1.03%, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index, versus 2.66% for the broad-based S&P 500 index. It was a relatively quiet three months for tech stocks, as the S&P 500 index was led by health care (+12%) and communication services (+7%), the only two sectors to outperform for the quarter. Technology finished fifth-best among the S&P 500's 11 equity sectors.

Technology stocks joined the broader market in extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April. The rally produced modest gains in the broader indexes during Q4 amid shifting sector leadership, an ongoing boom in spending for AI, a prolonged lack of economic data stemming from the federal government's shutdown, and two interest-rate cuts by the Federal Reserve.

Within the MSCI sector index, the semiconductor materials & equipment group (+19%) stood out for its strong performance. As expectations firmed up for robust semiconductor demand in coming quarters, chip equipment and materials stocks responded with outsized gains. Lam Research (LRCX)(+28%) and Applied Materials (AMAT)(+26%) were representative of this market segment.

On the other hand, software stocks struggled, as evidenced by the -10% return of the systems software group and -6% for application software, two segments with a large representation in the MSCI index. Systems software – represented by Microsoft (MSFT)(-6%) and Oracle (ORCL)(-31%), among others – was hurt by increased skepticism about the potential return on investment from the huge sums being plowed into generative AI. Meanwhile, application software stocks were pressured by fear that AI models might usurp many of the capabilities of software applications. Strategy (MSTR)(-53%), Cadence Design Systems (CDNS)(-11%), and Datadog (DDOG)(-5%) were members of this group. ■

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 1.75%, topping the MSCI U.S. IMI Information Technology 25/50 Index but trailing the broad-based S&P 500® index. Looking a bit longer term, performance comparisons over the trailing one- and 3-year periods favor the fund versus the MSCI sector index.

Versus the MSCI index, stock selection in technology hardware, storage & peripherals meaningfully aided performance. Positioning in systems software and a large underweight in application software also helped. An overweight in Micron Technology led our list of individual relative contributors versus the MSCI sector index. The stock gained 71% in Q4, rising in mid-December after the maker of memory chips for artificial intelligence and other applications reported strong financial results, including record revenue, for the three months ending November 27. Looking ahead, management said it expects business performance to continue strengthening through August, calling the company "an essential AI enabler" and noting growing demand for high-bandwidth memory for AI servers.

Underweighting Oracle (-31%) also contributed. The stock of the software and cloud-services provider fell after an October 16 business update seemed to raise questions about the potential profitability of the company's plan to capitalize on artificial intelligence by renting servers to AI companies. Shares slumped again in December after Oracle reported favorable financial results for the three months ending November 30 but also announced higher projected spending on AI and a firm-wide financial outlook that came in short of estimates.

Conversely, underweighting semiconductor materials & equipment notably detracted this quarter. In stock-specific terms, a sizable underweight in Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)(+32%) detracted more than any other holding. In early November, the semiconductor manufacturer reported better-than-expected earnings for the third quarter, driven by solid growth in its gaming and client businesses, and surging sales in its data-center segment. Management raised its revenue guidance for the fourth quarter. We favored other stocks with AI exposure, especially Nvidia (NVDA).■

Outlook and Positioning

Generative artificial intelligence continues to dominate the themes driving the tech sector, resulting in a somewhat bifurcated market between stocks the market perceives as "AI winners" and "AI losers." Consequently, many of the portfolio's largest overweights as of December 31 were semiconductor firms with AI ties. We continue to analyze the companies in the portfolio that fall into this AI basket, as well as makers of non-AI chips positioned to benefit from favorable cyclical shifts.

The fund's top industry overweight at quarter end – by a wide margin – was semiconductors, and two of the three biggest individual overweights were Nvidia and Marvell Technology (MRVL). companies that have strong ties to generative AI. We believe Nvidia is virtually in a class by itself because of its considerable head start over competitors and its "full-stack" approach of providing graphics processing hardware, along with AI-specific software and algorithms. The fund's sizable overweight in Nvidia reflects that view. The fund also continues to hold non-AI semiconductor stocks that are more cyclical, but which we believe could benefit if the economic environment improves and the inventory glut in non-AI chips eases. Fund holdings in this category include ON Semiconductor (ON), GlobalFoundries (GFS) and NXP Semiconductors (NXPI).

With that said, we're covering the tech sector as a whole and continue to invest in other themes that are impacting the sector, such as cloud computing, digital transformation and digital security. As always, we'll continue focusing on situations where our analysis diverges from the consensus view and look for opportunities within the sector that we believe offer the best relative risk/reward.

As we look at the fund's investment universe, we are trying to maintain our focus on stocks with a solid fundamental underpinning and avoid those where the fundamentals are questionable. For example, some investors worry that the Nvidia trade has become too crowded. However, even though the stock rose 39% for 2025, the firm's earnings increased more than that, which means that the valuation compressed during the year, as measured by the price-to-earnings ratio. On the other hand, there are stocks whose price increase has raced far ahead of earnings, leaving them vulnerable to a sharp pullback. Those are the ones we're trying to avoid.

We'll flag two noteworthy changes in the portfolio during Q4. We meaningfully reduced the fund's non-benchmark stake in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM). This was a case of "love the company, don't love the stock." Although TSMC saw its earnings post a strong gain in 2025, we saw little scope for positive surprises in the foreseeable future.

On the other hand, we added significantly to Marvell Technology, making it the fund's third-largest overweight as of December 31. The stock roughly broke even for the quarter and pulled back in December after a strong start to the month. The catalyst for the sell-off was the news that the company's stock had been downgraded due to Marvell's loss of a key piece of business with Amazon.com to a Taiwanese competitor. We saw this as a temporary headwind and continued to have strong conviction about the firm's longer-term prospects.

Thank you for your confidence in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities. ■

data.title: High Market Uncertainty: I Am Sleeping Well With Dividend Income
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Since its humble beginnings in the late 19th century, the visual art of movies has evolved significantly. This form of visual entertainment is designed to pull you into the story that is unfolding on the screen. When the visual effects and cinematography are done well, the experience can feel incredibly real. Take, for example, the 1984 sci-fi cult classic “The Terminator,” starring Arnold Schwarzenegger. The climax has one of the film’s most memorable scenes, where the severely damaged cyborg continues crawling toward Sarah Connor. Despite only having its upper body remaining, the relentless machine drags itself forward, inch by inch. The effects are so well done that the audience is part of the tension of the scene. It appears as though the cyborg could grab you at any moment.

Scenes like that put the audience on the edge of their seats. The fiction feels real, and that’s the brilliance of great filmmaking.

Financial markets operate similarly. Audience (investors) experience is often governed by alarming headlines, volatile price swings, and endless predictions, almost making it feel like you must constantly react to the next threat. Market narratives arrive daily, and if you try to respond to every development, investing can quickly become exhausting.

When you get paid for simply staying invested in the markets, market volatility becomes an opportunity. At High Dividend Opportunities, we bank on resilience and reliable passive income from our portfolio. In that spirit, we will look at two diversified funds that provide generous monthly income while helping keep market drama away from your financial future.

Pick #1: JPC – Yield 9.5%

Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) is a fixed-income-focused CEF that operates with floating-rate leverage. The fund maintains 90% exposure to institutional preferreds, a difficult-to-access asset class, making it a highly desired diversifier from the other fixed-income CEFs we hold in our portfolio. The CEF also maintains a nearly even split of holdings based on geography, with ~53% of its portfolio being securities from U.S. issuers and the remaining from non-U.S. issuers.

JPC has 257 holdings, with its top 5 positions comprising only 8% of the invested assets. About 75% of the portfolio is from financial services companies, which happen to be the largest issuers of preferred securities. As a result of this industry exposure, 79% of the portfolio is rated investment grade.

JPC also has a fairly staggered call exposure, protecting the income stream against a string of redemptions that can be expected with rate cuts.

JPC is yet to recover its lost NAV from the steepest rate hikes seen in modern history, but since the pause in the Fed’s rate hikes, followed by modest cuts, we have seen steady NAV appreciation.

We expect more of this as interest rates continue to move in the downward direction. JPC operates with a variable-rate leverage at OBFR+0.85%, and during FY 2025, the fund reported a weighted-average interest rate of 5.76%. With rate cuts, JPC’s borrowing cost will decline, resulting in a larger sum available for distributions.

Pick #2: RQI – Yield 9.1%

REITs ended 2025 on a strong note, with sector occupancy at 95.7%, led by retail and industrials. Office properties were notable laggards at 86% but marked two quarters of growth, indicating that we may have passed the bottom in this sub-sector last year.

As of September 30, 2025, REIT balance sheets were strong with low leverage ratios and debt-to-market assets at 32.9%, indicating that for $1 of property they own, they only owe about $0.33 in debt. The sector weighted-average term to maturity was 6.2 years, and the weighted average interest rate on debt was a modest 4.1%. REITs have successfully navigated the period of elevated borrowing costs and remain well-positioned to thrive with rate cuts in 2026.

Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund Inc (RQI) is a CEF (closed-end fund) with 190 holdings, where the top ten account for ~48% of its invested assets. These are among the biggest and most prominent REITs in the world, across healthcare, data centers, telecom, and industrial properties. Source

Welltower Inc. (WELL) is RQI’s top position, representing 11.3% of its invested assets. This has been a top-performing REIT in 2025, owning 3,000 properties in the United States, Canada, and the United Kingdom, comprising seniors housing, post-acute care, and wellness communities—well-positioned for the aging demographics in these nations.

RQI has been in existence since 2002, and the fund has excelled in making prudent allocations, as seen from a steady NAV over the past decade, despite operating through multiple cycles of rate hikes and cuts.

RQI has been a steady monthly payer, delivering $0.09/share, representing an 8.4% yield, with no return of capital in the past five years. RQI operates with ~30% leverage, with 81% of it carrying fixed rates with a weighted-average rate of 2.5% for a weighted-average term of 1.7 years.

REITs entered 2026 as an undervalued asset class. According to 2nd Market Capital, the average REIT trades at a 16% discount to NAV, and with RQI, you get a team of experts to actively manage a diversified REIT portfolio, paying you steady monthly income.

Conclusion

Like the cyborg crawling toward Sarah Connor, creating one of cinema’s most tense moments, financial headlines tend to create that same sense of relentless urgency. The difference is that, as investors, we don’t need to react to every moment of suspense. A well-built income portfolio lets us step away from the drama and focus on the steady stream of cash flow.

Our Income Method ensures that you don’t need to outrun the market’s drama; you can quietly collect dividends while the story unfolds. This is the beauty of Income Investing.

data.title: WhiteFiber: Look Past Q4 Noise For The $865M NC-1 Inflection Point
data.description:
Introduction

WhiteFiber's (WYFI) shares are down 60% since its highs in October 2025. The stock was once trading at close to $40 per share, but the share price underwent a serious correction following the fading of its IPO enthusiasm and concerns of an industry-wide AI bubble. Today, the share price is trading below its IPO price of $17 per share, which I view as a BUY opportunity for the stock.

However, I still would recommend a BUY call, which is premised on a few key reasons: First, the company is ramping up the development of its largest NC-1 data center site, which is expected to be a significant driver of earnings going forward. Second, the company has secured a major $865 million contract win with Nscale, which will begin contributing to WhiteFiber's revenue in Q2 FY2026. Lastly, WhiteFiber's shares are trading at an attractive valuation of just 5.3x FY2026 EV/EBITDA, which is a significant discount to its peers, who are trading at more than 20x FY2026 EV/EBITDA.

Although Q4 earnings fell short of consensus expectations, which caused the stock to tumble further post-results, this is just a minor setback, as monetization from its main NC-1 data center has yet to arrive. Based on the Q4 earnings call, management is expecting to begin monetizing this site around April or May FY2026, with a significant ramp in revenue expected in Q2 FY2026.

Business Overview

WhiteFiber is a Tier 3 HPC data center operator based in Montreal, Canada. The company also provides GPU and colocation services to customers for AI and Machine Learning applications. WhiteFiber was a former subsidiary under Bit Digital (which is a Bitcoin Mining/Ethereum Treasury company), but Bit Digital chose to list WhiteFiber to pursue data center opportunities, which is a rapidly booming market. Today, Bit Digital still owns a large stake in WhiteFiber, as they still retain 70% of all outstanding shares.

What sets WhiteFiber apart is its Tier 3 Data Center status; this means that WhiteFiber has to maintain near-perfect uptime for its customers. To maintain this standard, WhiteFiber has to have an uptime of 99.982%, which implies they have to have no more than 1.6 hours of downtime in a year. WhiteFiber is also differentiating itself through its software capabilities - the company has its own software layer known as WhiteFiber Cloud AI, which is used to link GPU clusters across multiple sites, as long as they are within an 80km radius.

These are all small plus points for WhiteFiber, but what are most important for WhiteFiber currently are their data center capacity and customer contracts.

Steady and Clear Expansion Plans for its Data Center Business

Over the past two years, management has been pursuing its Data Center strategy by acquiring and developing new data center sites across Montreal, Canada. In 2025, the key company has commissioned three key data center sites: MTL-1, MTL-2, and MTL-3. But most of WhiteFiber's revenue has not been coming from the data centers it owns. Instead, WhiteFiber is mainly using third-party colocation services in Iceland, meaning that its GPUs are deployed in third-party data centers, which WhiteFiber then leases out. Currently, 90% of WhiteFiber's revenue is still coming from cloud revenue that uses third-party colocation.

Even so, I am expecting the mix to drastically shift starting from 2026. Currently, WhiteFiber is only generating 10% of its revenue from its own colocation services/data centers. This is mainly stemming from its existing MTL-1 site, which is a 4 MW facility that was already operational in 2024. We will see more colocation services revenue heading into 2026, as management has just completed the development of its new MTL-3 data center, which will add another 7 MW capacity. Management is guiding a monthly run rate of $1 million from this new facility, which will more than double its existing colocation services revenue.

Major Data Center Project Development for Nscale

Although these data center projects are supporting WhiteFiber's growth, their contribution is small relative to WhiteFiber's new major data center site. Over the past year, WhiteFiber has been focusing on building a major 40 MW data center in North Carolina (NC-1). The capacity from this data center alone is larger than all of WhiteFiber's other data centers combined. While the development is not yet completed, management is expecting NC-1 to begin generating revenue starting May 2026 and to be the significant driver of revenue going forward for the company.

Currently, WhiteFiber has managed to secure an anchor customer for NC-1. WhiteFiber has signed an $865 million contract with Nscale; in return, WhiteFiber will provide colo services to Nscale over a 10-year duration for 40 MW of total capacity. This means that WhiteFiber will generate approximately $865 million/10 years = $86.5 million per year in revenue from this site, and the full capacity from this site will be completely taken up by Nscale.

Even so, I think that WhiteFiber will still be able to extract more value from this site, given that its contract with Duke Energy indicates that the site can be expanded to a maximum energy capacity of 99MW (based on the current agreement) and is feasible to receive 200MW of energy; however, more infrastructure upgrades are necessary to achieve this upper limit.

Figure 1: WhiteFiber Nscale Deal Announcement, Source: WhiteFiber

Total Revenue Projection for WhiteFiber

To assess the attractiveness of WhiteFiber's expansion plan, we have to first assess the financial impact that these expansion plans would bring to the company (MTL-1, MTL-2, MTL-3, and NC-1). The good thing about the company is that management has provided us with many clues from its S-1 filing and earnings call, which we can use to calculate this.

Based on the projects at hand. WhiteFiber is generating roughly $1.7 - 2.2 million in revenue per MW of data center capacity annually. In WhiteFiber's IPO prospectus, management was initially guiding for about $1.6 million per MW of capacity; however, from looking at its current deals/run-rate of its projects, the actual results have exceeded management's guidance.

Here is the calculation for the annualized revenue for each site: MTL-1 is calculated by extrapolating management's current colocation revenue in Q3 ($1.72 million x 4 = $7 million). MTL-3 is calculated by extrapolating management's guidance for a $1 million per month revenue in its earnings call. NC-1 revenue is based on its Nscale contract, which was calculated earlier.

Figure 2: List of WhiteFiber's Major Data Center Projects, Source: WhiteFiber S1 filing

Site

Capacity (MW)

AnnualizedRevenue

Completion

Revenue/MW

MTL-1

4.0

MTL-3

7.0

40.0

May 2026

$2.18 million

Based on the cadence provided by management, WhiteFiber is currently targeting to achieve a total of 76 MW in total capacity by the end of 2026. If we stick to management's guidance of $1.6 million per MW, this would imply 76 x $1.6 million = about $122 million in annualized revenue once this goal is reached. Management is also targeting a 75% EBITDA margin based on its colocation services, which would imply an EBITDA of $92 million at this expected run rate.

On top of this, we would also have to add the existing $17 million in Cloud services revenue, for which management is also targeting a 75-80% EBITDA margin. Altogether, this would bring the total annualized revenue for WhiteFiber to $139 million, which would generate about $105 million in EBITDA.

Financials

The primary concern I think most investors would have is how the company is financing its current expansion plans. Surprisingly, most of the development costs have been financed with funds from Bit Digital and from the funds raised during WhiteFiber's IPO ($160 million raised). As of Q4 FY2025, the company still has $114 million in cash on hand. Offsetting this, there is only $23 million in interest-bearing debt (mainly lease liabilities). This implies that WhiteFiber has a net cash of $91 million, which alleviates the debt concerns. Management has $8 million in development costs per MW, meaning that its current cash position is sufficient to finance about 10 MW of further expansion without the need to raise additional funds.

This is obviously not enough, as management is expanding capacity by more than 70 MW this year. Recently in January, the company has just raised an additional $230 million in senior convertible notes to finance its expansion. However, I am not too concerned, given that the interest on these bonds is 4.5% against management's 7% disposition cap rate guidance for its data centers.

Figure 3: Selected Financials from WhiteFiber's Latest 10-K Filing, Source: WhiteFiber's 10-K

Valuation

Based on consensus estimates, WhiteFiber's valuations do not seem expensive, as the stock is trading at 5.3x FY2026 EV/EBITDA. For FY2026, consensus is projecting $169 million in revenue and EBITDA of $83 million. It seems that consensus is projecting higher revenue than my $139 million revenue forecast but lower than my $105 million in EBITDA forecast. This could be explained by consensus using a higher revenue/MW and forecasting some growth in WhiteFiber's cloud services business, as I am taking a more conservative assumption on these aspects. But my EBITDA forecast is higher than consensus, which may be because I am using management's 75% EBITDA margin guidance, which is only achievable when the project matures. Overall, my forecasts are not too far off from consensus projections, and EV/EBITDA would be around the 5x range even using my projections.

Figure 4: Consensus Estimates for WhiteFiber (FY2026), Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Compared to its data center peers, WhiteFiber is trading at a large valuation discount, as its 5.3x FY2026 EV/EBITDA is a stark discount compared to its competitors, such as Applied Digital (APLD), Digital Realty (DLR), and Equinix (EQIX), which are trading at more than 20x FY2026 EV/EBITDA. This highlights potential execution concerns by investors, as WhiteFiber was previously owned by a digital asset company with little experience in executing large data center projects. However, I do not think this to be the case; otherwise, why would a Hyperscaler like Nscale commit to such a large contract with WhiteFiber?

Figure 5: Peer Analysis of WhiteFiber and Key Metrics, Source: Quartr (Data by S&P Global)

Company Market Cap (USD) EV/EBITDA (2026) P/S (2026)
WhiteFiber 562 5.3 3.3
Applied Digital 7,900 26.7 22.8
Digital Realty 60,600 22.0 9.0
Equinix 94,800 22.3 9.3

Risk to the Company

There are several key risks that could materially affect the BUY thesis. Although I am not expecting these scenarios to happen in the base case of my analysis, they could pose a material downside if they occur. The most significant risk would be the risk of contract termination from WhiteFiber's largest customers. WhiteFiber is reliant on its contracts with Nscale, Cerebras, and other customers to generate colocation revenue and monetize its data center capacity. If there is any termination in contracts by any of these parties, it could materially affect WhiteFiber's revenues.

Another key risk would be potential counterparty risk. Neo-cloud players like Nscale are engaging in aggressive capex/data center leasing commitments, which are often financed through debt. If Nscale faces financial difficulties from its expansion plan, it could pose material risk to WhiteFiber.

Lastly, there is a risk that WhiteFiber is unable to source sufficient clients to fully utilize its data center capacity. WhiteFiber is currently expanding its existing data center capacity, and as WhiteFiber scales, they will have to source additional clients. If they are unable to accomplish this, it would also result in a negative impact on the company's financials.

Conclusion

Overall, based on the reasons I have outlined, I think that WhiteFiber is a Buy. First, on valuations, the stock is trading at FY2026 EV/EBITDA, which is a discount to its peers in the data center provider space. Although WhiteFiber may be disadvantaged in scale compared to its competitors like Applied Digital and Digital Realty, there is strong demand for data center capacity, which means that differentiation is of secondary importance in this industry. The limitation faced by hyperscalers currently is land, power, and servers - which means that as long as WhiteFiber can provide all of these and maintain limited downtime in its operations, they are poised to capture this data center opportunity, as demand far outpaces supply. The clear catalyst for the stock will be the completion of the NC-1 40MW data center, which is expected in H2 2026. So far the construction is proceeding on track, and management is keeping its original development timeline. Although this may be a high-risk play since there will be concentration risk from Nscale, I think WhiteFiber still warrants a BUY from me.

data.title: Vail Resorts: A Bad Snow Year Doesn't Change The Thesis
data.description:
Vail Resorts (MTN) has seen its share price punished by a lofty valuation along with the 2025-2026 ski season coming in as one of the worst in a decade+. Despite that, the company's intelligent moves for 2026-2027, along with an owned portfolio of top-tier ski assets, position Vail Resorts well to maintain its almost 7% dividend and strong shareholder returns.

Vail Resorts

Vail Resorts operates one of the most impressive portfolios in the ski industry, with 42 resorts and >2 million pass holders.

This massive integrated portfolio enables strong synergies as the company continues to work to build an integrated and profitable experience. At the same time, these assets are irreplaceable, with no major ski resorts built in decades. That means as demand grows, it'll be driven to Vail's existing resorts, improving margins in a high fixed-cost business.

Vail has continued to improve experiences despite the impact of the Park City Mountain labor union strike, one of the company's largest resorts, followed by a poor winter. This was a hurdle, but one that's past the company, and the net concessions from the union fight are not prohibitively expensive.

Vail has built the Epic Pass model with 2+ million Epic Pass holders driving 74% of visits and 64% of revenue in advance. That enables the company to handle season-to-season volatility better, with the $1 billion in Epic Pass revenue. That's incredibly key to an industry that has high fixed costs, and it's key to Vail's future success.

Vail Resorts Financial Results

Financially, the company's performance is seen below. Despite a 28% decline in snowfall and a 10% decline in visits, adjusted EBITDA stayed flat.

Similarly, the 2025-2026 season snowfall is incredibly weak, but we expect the company to be able to handle this. The company's resort reported EBITDA has stayed flat for the past few years, but the company has handled the impact of COVID-19 well. We expect Vail Resorts to be able to handle the weak 2025-2026 snowfall as well.

Vail Resorts has spent $2 billion in capital, $1.9 billion in dividends, and $0.9 billion in share repurchases over the past decade. That has put the company at a higher net debt position of ~$2.5 billion. At the same time, its share price decline since the above slide has pushed its dividend up to almost 7%.

Still, the company's dividend of less than $320 million on an annual basis is comfortably affordable. At the same time, with the Fed still expected to cut rates in coming years, that could help the company to manage interest expenses.

Vail Resorts Forecast

Vail Resorts is focused on continuing to increase revenue, and we think it's made some great decisions. In 2026-2027, pass offerings include a 20% discount for young adults aged 18-30, something that we think will attract new skiers to the resort and Epic Pass, based on anecdotal conversations with skiers and their families.

Epic has substantially increased pass revenue, even when accounting for some difficult winters, the impact of COVID-19, and the company's pricing reset. We expect the company to not grow at 16% annually on this front, but we do expect it to continue modest growth. Keep in mind, skiing is a high fixed cost industry, so reliable revenue here is key to the company's long-term success.

Still, skiing is ultimately a hobby, and a tough economy is a tough economy. We expect the pass to help Epic to be able to weather low snow years and lower visits, but what we expect it to struggle with is still a scenario such as the global financial crisis, where a recession causes people to substantially cut back on spending.

With skyrocketing oil prices threatening to cause such a recession, that is a major risk.

However, building loyalty among pass guests, especially given the low marginal costs for additional guests, has pushed down customer acquisition costs and substantially increased return rates. Those reliable commitments, customers who purchase a pass every year, are key to the company's future ability to provide reliable returns.

At the same time, Epic Pass has the ability to monetize auxiliary services. Only 6% of guests have taken ski school, and only 16% of guests currently rent with Vail Resorts. These auxiliary businesses provide just over $400 million in revenue, and they're both high-margin businesses. At the same time, on-mountain, especially for ski and ride school, there's no real competition.

That makes it a great opportunity for Epic Pass to grow these services, while providing value. Expansion in these services, given low existing market penetration, would be huge for future success. Again, it's worth noting that Vail Resorts has a high fixed-fee business, so any auxiliary growth here is all high margin.

Vail Returns

Vail has continued to generate strong FCF with a high conversion rate.

The company has moved to a more than 50% FCF conversion rate, maintaining high FCF. The company saw a substantial COVID-19 related dip; however, it has recovered substantially. We expect the company to be able to continue maintaining this FCF recovery. That'll enable Vail to maintain its dividend, invest in its business, and have strong returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is that skiing is a luxury activity. With the potential of a global recession from higher oil prices, that could cause a drop-off in the company's performance, which could hurt its momentum. That could substantially hurt Vail's ability to continue driving shareholder returns, making it a poor investment.

Conclusion

Vail Resorts has an incredibly strong portfolio of assets in skiing. It's a nearly irreplaceable portfolio of assets, with no major new ski resort developments in decades. Vail's positioning has enabled the company to generate a strong cash flow yield, enabling an almost 7% dividend and strong performance from its portfolio.

At the same time, Vail Resorts has substantial opportunity. The Epic Pass is providing the company with reliable revenue in a high fixed cost business. At the same time, the company has substantial opportunity from lessons and gear rentals, which will provide the company with growing cash flow. That makes Vail Resorts a valuable investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Objective Use for Morningstar category
Attractive risk-adjusted returns One-stop investment for 529 education savings Target-Enrollment 2027

Highlights

  • U.S. stocks extended a historically fast rebound that began in early April, although upward momentum slowed in the final quarter of the year.
  • The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI, and the Fed's interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December.
  • Healthcare, a laggard for much of 2025, was the top-performing sector in the S&P 500 Index during Q4.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks gained 2.66% in the fourth quarter, according to the S&P 500 Index, extending a historically fast rebound that began in early April, but at a slower pace. The advance was supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on artificial intelligence, and the U.S. Federal Reserve's (Fed's) interest rate reductions of 0.25% in October and December. Amid this favorable backdrop for higher-risk assets, the index closed the year just shy of its all-time high. Value stocks modestly outpaced growth in Q4, while large caps had only a slight advantage over small caps, as investors' appetite for risk waned versus the prior three months.

In the fourth quarter, the index's narrow advance was driven by the defensive-oriented healthcare sector (+12%), which particularly shined in November. The growth-oriented communication services sector rose about 7%, benefiting from the transformative potential of generative AI. All other groups lagged the index. Notably, IT, which represented 35% of the S&P 500 Index in Q4, rose about 1%.

International developed-market stocks advanced 4.89%, as measured by the MSCI EAFE Index. The discrepancy between value and growth stocks was more pronounced in this index, again in favor of value over growth. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index notched a 4.72% rise. South Korea (+27%) and Taiwan (+10%) were two strong components with sizable representation in this index, bolstered by the strength in technology stocks in those markets. On the other hand, China finished the quarter with a roughly -7% return, giving back some of its gains since the April bottom.

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 1.1% in the fourth quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Quarterly performance was fueled by a combination of elevated starting yields and price appreciation, as bond prices rose amid falling interest rates and investor demand, supported by stable fundamentals.

The U.S. equity bull market is entering its fourth year, having put up double-digit gains in each of the past three, leaving investors wondering whether it can keep the streak alive. While multiyear stretches of positive equity returns are common, double-digit returns four years in row are few and far between. The naysayers point to elevated valuations, narrowness of the market, uncertainty around Fed policy amid upcoming leadership change, a softening labor market, and U.S. mid-term election risk. Yet, history has shown that bull markets don't die of old age alone, and while valuations are elevated, they're below prior cycle extremes. Bulls can also point to still-robust earnings expectations, a broadening of AI beneficiaries, fiscal support, increased capital spending, deregulation, rising mergers-and-acquisitions activity, and easing trade tensions. With the risks reasonably balanced and momentum still intact, the path of least resistance for the equity market may continue to be higher for now. The question is, can it reach double digits for the four-peat?

Contributors and detractors

The portfolio recorded a single-digit gain for the quarter and trailed its blended benchmark, comprising a 19.95% weighting in the Russell 3000 Index, a 8.55% weighting in the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index (net), a 45.00% weighting in the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and a 26.50% weighting in the Bloomberg 1-5 Year TIPS Index. Disciplined Value International Fund (John Hancock) was a noteworthy detractor for the quarter. The fund's lower allocation to value stocks and higher allocation to growth versus the MSCI EAFE Value Index was a headwind in a quarter where international value equities strongly outperformed growth names. Additionally, the portfolio's exposure to a hedged equity strategy detracted, as stock indexes moved higher, closing out a strong year. Conversely, Spectrum Income (T. Rowe Price) contributed modestly on a relative basis, largely due to our out-of-benchmark exposure to emerging-market bonds (both dollar-denominated and local currency). International Growth Fund (John Hancock) also contributed, aided by security selection in the financials sector. A tactical overweight to international value equities further contributed, given the relatively strong performance of that group.
